source_cards.spark: A 1960s mathematical technique for inventing new theories by blending two partial, incomplete ones turns out to share the exact skeleton of what your system is trying to do — not because the domains are similar, but because the underlying shape of 'productive incompleteness resolving into coherent form' is identical.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor is the user's half-formed pursuit — not a finished idea, but a skeleton of intent, enough to recognize resonance when it appears. That is exactly the role the partial axiomatizations play in the paper: they are not complete theories, they are just enough structure to constrain what a valid blend could look like. The colimit computation corresponds to your retrospective resonance scoring — both are mechanisms that look at two incomplete things and ask 'what minimal coherent structure contains both?' The paper's 'weakening to resolve inconsistency' step maps onto your articulation engine's job: when a cross-domain find doesn't quite fit, you don't reject it, you find the looser framing under which it does cohere. And the output in both cases is inheritance without repetition — a new structure that descends from its inputs but is not reducible to either.
source_cards.source_shape: The paper models mathematical invention using conceptual blending: two formal theories, each partially axiomatized, are combined through a colimit computation — a category-theory operation that finds the minimal structure that contains both inputs without contradiction. When the blend produces inconsistencies, the system weakens constraints until coherence is restored, and the result is a new theory that neither input theory contained. The key move is that neither input needs to be complete — the blend is what forces the gaps to resolve.
source_cards.use: Consider treating the user's anchor not as a filter that accepts or rejects wandering finds, but as one input space in a blend — the find is the second input, and your articulation layer's job is to compute the minimal coherent bridge between them, weakening constraints until the connection holds rather than discarding near-misses.
source_cards.limit: The paper's input spaces are precise CASL specifications — fully formal, machine-readable first-order logic theories where every term is defined and every axiom is explicit. Your system is designed to receive the opposite: vague, possibly non-mathematical, half-articulated pursuits that cannot be formalized before the process begins. The colimit computation is also deterministic — given the same two input theories, it always produces the same blend. Your wandering is designed to be genuinely unpredictable across arbitrary domains like theater or biology, which means the clean computational guarantees the paper relies on have no foothold here. The bridge holds for the shape of productive incompleteness resolving into coherent form, but breaks entirely at the question of how you run that resolution when the inputs refuse to be formalized.

source_cards.spark: A method for teaching mathematics by mapping prerequisite knowledge as a fixed hierarchy accidentally reveals what your system must NOT be — and in doing so, sharpens exactly what it must be instead.
source_cards.bridge: Your system also deals in inheritance — the idea that a concept someone is working on carries structural echoes of prior work and partial knowing. That surface resemblance is what the agent noticed. But the contrast is where the real signal lives: Gagné's hierarchy is a closed world, drawn by experts before any exploration begins, while your system's skeleton must emerge from the wandering itself — the structure is discovered, not pre-declared. The Gagné model shows you a fully articulated spine; your system needs a spine that assembles itself retrospectively, from resonance found across chaotic traversal. Seeing the rigid version clearly makes the requirement for softness and emergence more precise, not just different in degree but different in kind.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy treats knowledge as a directed tree: before a student can learn concept B, they must fully possess concept A, because A is a structural prerequisite of B. Software built on this model encodes that tree explicitly — every path through the curriculum is pre-specified by domain experts before any learner arrives. The system knows all valid routes in advance; it simply guides each learner along the one that fits their current position in the tree.
source_cards.use: You might use this contrast as a design test: for any architectural constraint you add to guide the agents, ask whether an expert could have written it down before any wandering happened — if yes, it is Gagné-shaped and probably too rigid for what you need.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné hierarchy is a fully specified, expert-authored prerequisite tree that determines every possible path before a single learner touches the system; there is no discovery, only traversal of a known map. Your system's structure must emerge from chaotic wandering and retrospective resonance — the skeleton is not designed in advance, it is found. The source's 'inheritance' is deterministic code reuse between pre-defined nodes, not the open-ended detection of non-obvious structural matches across unrelated domains, so the bridge holds only as a contrast that clarifies your requirements, not as a model to borrow from.

selection.why_these_cards: P04-002 gives a precise mechanism for resolving incompleteness — colimit blending that weakens constraints until coherence holds. P01-003 is the sharpest anti-model: Gagné as the thing the system must NOT be, with a concrete test (could an expert have written this down beforehand?). Colliding the formal-resolution engine against the explicit prohibition on pre-declared structure forces a question neither card answers alone.
selection.spark: P04-002 says 'weaken constraints until the blend coheres.' P01-003 says 'the spine must assemble retrospectively, never pre-declared.' If you weaken constraints, you are loosening a structure — but where did the structure to loosen come from if nothing is pre-declared?
selection.motive: The cushion's hardest sub-problem is articulating a vague inspiration without a formal spec. This blend produces the actual articulation mechanism: how to make a half-formed thing cohere without ever formalizing it first.
selection.tension: Colimit blending REQUIRES fully axiomatized inputs to compute the minimal coherent containing structure. The user's anchor REFUSES formalization by design. These cannot both hold — formal resolution needs formal inputs, but the system's value forbids them.
selection.discovery_path: P04-002 asserts coherence is reached by starting from constraints and weakening them. P01-003 asserts no constraints may be declared in advance. Both cannot hold unless the constraints themselves are GENERATED retrospectively from the wander, then weakened. Pursuing 'where do retrospective constraints come from' forced the question: what if the system runs blending BACKWARD — it observes a resonance hit (an already-cohered pair), then INFERS the minimal shared skeleton that must have made it cohere, rather than computing the blend forward from declared axioms. The thesis is the answer to 'how do you get colimit-style coherence guarantees without colimit-style formal inputs.'

blends.thesis: Retrospective colimit inference: the system never blends forward from formal inputs; it detects an emergent coherent pairing first, then runs the colimit logic in reverse to RECONSTRUCT the minimal shared skeleton that explains the coherence — turning the user's vague anchor into a formal-enough object only after a match is found, not before.
blends.mechanism: When a wander surfaces a resonance hit between anchor and source, the articulation layer does not ask 'do these blend?' — it assumes they blended (resonance is the evidence) and solves for the generalization space that must exist between them. That inferred skeleton is the articulation: it is the precise statement of what the user's vague inspiration actually was, now legible because a concrete cross-domain instance forced it into shape. The 'weakening' step becomes a tuning knob: a looser inferred skeleton accepts more distant resonances; a tighter one demands closer matches.
blends.emergent_structure: Formalization as an OUTPUT of matching rather than a PRECONDITION for it — the anchor becomes well-specified only retroactively, with the matched source acting as the chisel that carves the user's vague intent into stated form. Neither card has this: P04-002 requires formal inputs upfront; P01-003 only diagnoses what to avoid and offers no constructive resolution mechanism.
blends.advances_cushion: Directly solves the 'I have vague inspirations but can't articulate it' half of the cushion: the user never has to articulate first. They gesture; the system finds a resonant instance; the act of explaining WHY it resonates is itself the articulation of their inspiration.

source_cards.spark: A physics benchmark for AI discovery reveals something counterintuitive: giving an agent more prior knowledge doesn't produce better exploration — it produces more constrained exploration. The right amount of prior is the one that biases without dictating, and that's a design principle, not a physics fact.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor plays exactly the role PhysGym's controlled prior plays. It doesn't tell the wandering agents what to surface — it biases which connections will feel resonant when they're found. The three-point anchor structure maps directly to the 'sparse prior' design: three points is enough to create a gravitational shape without collapsing the search space into a single expected answer. PhysGym also shows that varying the prior changes the discovery path — which confirms your system's implicit bet that a richer or more specific anchor would produce tighter but less surprising results. And the retrospective scoring mechanism your system uses (resonance judged after the wander, not during) mirrors PhysGym's evaluation logic: you can't score discovery in real time without destroying what makes it discovery.
source_cards.source_shape: PhysGym is a benchmark that tests how well LLMs discover physical laws when given different amounts of prior knowledge before they start exploring. The key finding is that the prior doesn't tell the agent what to find — it shapes which directions feel worth investigating. Too little prior and the agent wanders without traction; too much and it stops genuinely exploring, converging on what it already expects. The productive zone is a controlled sparse prior: enough structure to make some directions feel more resonant than others, not enough to predetermine the destination.
source_cards.use: Consider formalizing your anchor's three-point structure as a 'controlled prior specification' — explicitly defining what density of anchor input produces the productive incompleteness zone versus the over-determined zone. PhysGym's evaluation framework, which measures how prior density shifts discovery quality, could be borrowed almost directly to test whether your anchor is calibrated right: run the same wander with anchors of varying specificity and measure how the resonance distribution changes.
source_cards.limit: PhysGym operates entirely within physics — a domain with ground truth, where 'correct discovery' can be objectively scored against known laws. Your system is cross-domain and user-facing, where the target isn't a discoverable fact but a half-formed personal vision that doesn't yet have a clear shape even to the person holding it. PhysGym also starts from scratch with no prior human work to inherit — it has no analog for the user's existing project, accumulated context, or the way a vague inspiration is entangled with personal history. The bridge holds for the sparse-prior-enables-productive-exploration mechanism, but breaks completely at the question of how you score success when the destination is a crystallized human intention rather than a verified physical law.

source_cards.spark: A mathematics education tool maps student proficiency not by asking students everything, but by reading a sparse set of responses and inferring where they sit on a hidden landscape — the same move your system makes when it uses a thin anchor to navigate a vast conceptual space.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the role the construct map plays here: it defines the latent landscape without dictating the path through it. When a wandering agent takes a sparse input — a half-formed concept, a fragment of work — and navigates outward, it is doing the same thing as the scoring model: using a thin signal to find a position on a rich structure that wasn't explicitly pre-mapped. The retrospective resonance scoring your system uses (judging a find's relevance after the wander, not before) mirrors exactly how construct map placement works — the score is assigned after the response is observed, not predicted in advance. The structural insight is that a well-designed anchor doesn't need to anticipate every destination; it just needs to be coherent enough that placement becomes legible once the agent returns.
source_cards.source_shape: Construct maps are structured scales that represent a latent trait — in this case, mathematical proficiency — as a continuous landscape with ordered levels. Researchers don't observe the trait directly; they infer it from a small number of carefully chosen student responses, then score where each student lands retrospectively, after the responses are in. The map is designed in advance, but the placement emerges from the data — the system reads sparse signals and resolves them into a rich, non-obvious position on the landscape.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your anchor design is doing the work a construct map does — not specifying destinations, but creating enough internal coherence that retrospective scoring is reliable. If resonance scores feel arbitrary or noisy, the anchor may lack the ordered structure that makes sparse-input navigation resolve cleanly.
source_cards.limit: Construct maps are deterministic, domain-locked, and exhaustively validated — they are blueprints, not skeletons. They work because mathematical proficiency is a well-understood, bounded trait with known levels and a stable scoring rubric. Your system requires genuine unpredictability and cross-domain wandering, which a construct map explicitly forecloses: it cannot discover structure it wasn't designed to represent. The bridge holds for the mechanism of sparse-input-to-rich-placement, but breaks entirely at the question of novelty — construct maps confirm known structure, they do not reveal unknown structure.

source_cards.spark: A system that breathes life into static physics diagrams by reading only their sparse visual bones — no instructions, no target specified in advance — and generating full interactive simulations from that minimum. The mechanism that makes this possible is structurally identical to the one your wandering agents use.
source_cards.bridge: Your agents face the same causal shape at the input end: the anchor is the sparse structural input — a minimal constraint that biases the search without specifying its destination. Just as Augmented Physics cannot be told in advance which physical system to find (because that would presuppose the answer), your agents cannot be handed a query-directed path without destroying the value of what they might surface. The 'segment-anything plus LLM scoring' step maps directly onto your resonance-scoring layer: both are mechanisms for reading structural pattern from sparse signal and assigning a confidence that something meaningful was found — without requiring the seeker to know what 'meaningful' looks like before the search begins. The key correspondence is that scoring happens retrospectively, after extraction, not prospectively as a filter — and that retrospective-only posture is precisely what allows structurally unexpected matches to survive long enough to be evaluated.
source_cards.source_shape: Augmented Physics takes a static textbook diagram and extracts just its structural components — lines, arrows, labels, shapes — using a segmentation model, then passes those sparse fragments to a multimodal LLM that scores what physical system they most likely represent, and finally generates a runnable simulation of that system. The system never asks the user 'what physics are you showing me?' It infers the category from structural pattern alone, then builds outward from that recognition. The pipeline runs extraction first, scoring second — it cannot know what it found until after it has looked.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your agents' resonance scoring could be made explicitly retrospective in the same way — running a 'what did I actually find?' pass after traversal rather than filtering candidates against a pre-specified target type, which would let structurally surprising matches reach scoring instead of being pruned before they arrive.
source_cards.limit: Augmented Physics is domain-locked to physics diagrams and always terminates at a known output category: a runnable simulation of a recognized physical system. There is no ambiguity about what a successful result looks like, and accuracy is measurable against ground truth (64%, 44%, and so on). Your system's value lives in the opposite condition — the output category must be unknown in advance, and arriving somewhere structurally unexpected is the point, not a failure mode. Augmented Physics also has no analog to a Markov-chain trajectory or a retrospective-only trail; its pipeline is deterministic and directed, which is precisely the architecture your mechanism layer identifies as destroying the target output. The bridge holds at the sparse-input-to-rich-output shape and the retrospective scoring posture — it breaks completely at the question of open-ended cross-domain traversal and the deliberate suppression of a known destination.

selection.why_these_cards: P02-001 gives the calibration insight (too much prior kills exploration, there is a productive density zone). P05-001 gives the construct-map insight (retrospective placement on a latent landscape requires enough internal coherence). S9cd764-002 gives the extraction-then-score ordering (you cannot know what you found until after you look). Three cards in tension over WHEN structure is allowed to act: before, never, or after.
selection.spark: P02-001 warns that a richer anchor produces 'tighter but less surprising' results — there is a measurable tradeoff curve. P05-001 says retrospective scoring only resolves cleanly if the anchor has enough ordered structure. These two are in direct conflict: more structure improves scoring reliability but reduces surprise. Where is the optimum?
selection.motive: The cushion needs a way to tune how far above 'the same level' the system pushes. The user explicitly says 'I don't want to be at the same level' — that is a request for a controllable advancement distance, which this blend operationalizes.
selection.tension: P02-001 says anchor richness trades surprise for tightness. P05-001 says scoring needs richness to be reliable. S9cd764-002 says scoring must happen AFTER extraction so surprises survive. If you add richness for reliable scoring, you kill the surprise that retrospective scoring was built to preserve.
selection.discovery_path: P02-001 establishes a surprise-vs-tightness curve driven by prior density. P05-001 says scoring reliability also rises with anchor structure. S9cd764-002 says scoring is retrospective precisely so surprises aren't pruned early. Holding all three: if richness simultaneously improves scoring AND kills surprise, then a single fixed anchor density cannot be optimal. Pursuing this forced the question: what if anchor density is SPLIT — sparse during traversal (to preserve surprise) and enriched only at the scoring step (to make placement reliable)? The thesis is the answer: a two-phase prior where the wander runs against a deliberately under-specified anchor but scoring runs against a retrospectively-enriched one.

blends.thesis: Asymmetric anchor density: the anchor the agents wander against is deliberately thinner than the anchor used to score what they find. Traversal sees a sparse prior (maximal surprise); scoring sees a richer prior (reliable placement) — and the GAP between the two densities is the system's tunable 'advancement distance' dial.
blends.mechanism: Maintain two representations of the same anchor. The wander uses the thin one so directions stay genuinely open. After a candidate returns, score it against the enriched representation, which carries the user's accumulated context and load-bearing certainties. Widening the gap (thinner wander-anchor, richer scoring-anchor) pushes the system to surface things further from the user's current level while still filtering for genuine inheritance. Narrowing it produces safe, incremental advances.
blends.emergent_structure: The advancement distance is a controllable parameter equal to the DIFFERENCE between two anchor densities — not a property of a single anchor. No source card has two anchors at different densities; each treats the anchor as one object with one density to be calibrated.
blends.advances_cushion: Gives the user direct control over 'how far past the existing work' the system reaches — the exact knob the cushion's 'I don't want to be at the same level' demands, while keeping surprises alive through the sparse wander-anchor.

source_cards.spark: A mathematics education system encodes skills as inherited layers — each higher concept legally 'owns' everything below it. Your system also tries to surface what prior work a new concept can stand on, but the mechanism by which inheritance happens turns out to be the opposite.
source_cards.bridge: The surface resemblance to your system is real: both try to answer the question 'what does this concept build on?' But the resemblance is where the comparison earns its value, because the mechanisms are inverted. In Gagné, the inheritance graph is fully known before the system runs — a human expert pre-draws every edge. In your system, the relationships are unknown until the wandering agent finds them; the whole point is that the structure cannot be pre-drawn without destroying the output. What the Gagné model does reveal is a clean articulation of what 'inheritance without repetition' means as a goal: a higher node should absorb the lower node's content without restating it, adding only the delta. That delta-only principle — advance by the gap, not by the whole — is a structural property your system might want to preserve even as the mechanism for discovering which gaps exist remains open and emergent.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills into a strict prerequisite chain: you cannot understand concept B until you have mastered concept A, because B is defined as A-plus-one-new-thing. The paper encodes this as binary inheritance vectors — a fixed blueprint where every node in the hierarchy knows exactly which lower nodes it depends on, decided in advance by a human designer. The result is a system that can diagnose gaps in a student's knowledge by checking which prerequisite rungs are missing, but only because the entire staircase was drawn before anyone climbed it.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your system's resonance scoring could be designed to surface the 'delta' — not just 'this prior work is related' but 'here is specifically what your concept adds that the prior work does not already contain' — so the user sees the inheritance edge and the new contribution simultaneously.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné system presupposes the entire hierarchy before a single student interacts with it: every node, every edge, every prerequisite is hard-coded by a designer. This is structurally incompatible with your system's core requirement — that the agent cannot know what it will find until it wanders there, and that pre-defining the structure would collapse the output. There is no chaotic traversal, no cross-domain surprise, and no soft anchoring in Gagné; it is a top-down instructional blueprint, and the inheritance it describes is fixed and deterministic rather than discovered and emergent. The bridge holds only for the abstract goal of 'advance by delta,' not for any part of the mechanism that gets you there.

source_cards.spark: A mathematics education system encodes every higher skill as 'everything the lower skill knew, plus one new thing' — your system could borrow that accumulation logic without borrowing the rigid ladder it runs on.
source_cards.bridge: The single point of contact is the inheritance-without-repetition principle: each level adds something new and carries everything prior forward, rather than restating it from scratch. Your system accumulates insights across wandering sessions — each new card, each new connection, builds on the conceptual mass already in play. The Gagné model names a mechanism for that accumulation: instead of insights piling up as a flat list, they could be encoded so that each new insight explicitly declares what prior insight it extends, making the inheritance visible and traceable. Where the math system uses this to build a diagnostic ladder, your system could use the same encoding to make the growth of a user's concept legible — not 'here are 40 cards' but 'here is what this concept has become, layer by layer.'
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills in a strict prerequisite chain: you cannot meaningfully learn skill B until you have mastered skill A, because B is defined as A plus one new cognitive operation. The 'inheritance coding' in the source formalizes this — each node in the hierarchy literally carries forward the content of all nodes below it, so nothing is re-taught and nothing is skipped. The result is a diagnostic system that can pinpoint exactly where a learner's chain broke, because the chain has a fixed, knowable shape.
source_cards.use: Consider whether each card or insight surfaced by the system could carry a lightweight 'extends' pointer — a link to the prior insight it builds on — so that over time the user can see not just what the system found, but how their concept has been accumulating structure rather than just accumulating content.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné hierarchy is deterministic and bottom-up: there is one correct order, lower skills are hard prerequisites for higher ones, and the entire system is designed to diagnose gaps in a fixed sequence. Your system's wandering is the opposite — chaotic, cross-domain, and only coherent in retrospect, with no predetermined order and no concept of a prerequisite. The source's inheritance works because the domain (mathematics skill acquisition) has a real, stable dependency graph; your domain does not, so the diagnostic and sequencing machinery of the source has no analog in your system. The inheritance-without-repetition shape is borrowable; the ladder it runs on is not.

source_cards.spark: A system that turns a single static diagram into a fully interactive physics simulation uses the same sparse-to-rich expansion logic your system relies on — a minimal, fixed input becomes a navigable space of possibilities.
source_cards.bridge: Your system takes a user's anchor — a minimal statement of intent — and expands it into a traversal across domains, surfacing structural resonances the user could not have searched for directly. That is the same sparse-to-rich shape: fixed, minimal input in; navigable, surprising output out. The 'skeleton-level understanding' the paper relies on maps directly to how your agents work — they recognize structural patterns and relationships without needing to fully simulate or understand the source domain from first principles. And just as Augmented Physics inherits from pretrained models rather than building physics knowledge from scratch, your agents inherit structural intuitions from whatever representation layer they use to detect resonance — they do not reconstruct each domain, they recognize shape within it.
source_cards.source_shape: Augmented Physics takes a static textbook diagram — one frozen image — and uses pretrained vision models (Segment Anything, multimodal LLMs) to identify objects, infer relationships, and inject interactive behavior, producing a simulation the reader can manipulate. The system does not derive physics from first principles; it recognizes structural skeletons — what objects are present, how they relate — and inherits the physics behavior from existing models. The user provides almost nothing beyond the diagram itself, and the system does the work of expanding that sparse input into something explorable.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your agents' resonance-detection layer could be made more explicit about what level of structural knowing it is operating at — skeleton (objects and relationships), blueprint (causal mechanism), or simulation (full behavior) — since the Augmented Physics pipeline suggests that skeleton-level recognition is often sufficient to produce genuinely useful, explorable output without requiring deeper domain comprehension.
source_cards.limit: Augmented Physics is a deterministic, single-domain pipeline: give it a physics diagram, get a physics simulation. There is no cross-domain wandering, no unpredictable traversal, and no retrospective trail of discovery — the system never surfaces a connection between, say, a thermodynamics diagram and a concept in music theory. The user's input is also concrete and visual, not an abstract intent or a three-point anchor acting as a tuning fork. The sparse-to-rich expansion shape holds, but everything downstream of that — the wandering, the domain-crossing, the structural resonance across unrelated fields — has no analog in this source at all.

selection.why_these_cards: P01-001 isolates the 'advance by the delta, not the whole' principle. P04-004 adds the 'extends' pointer — making each insight declare what prior insight it builds on. P06-001 adds the skeleton/blueprint/simulation levels of structural knowing. Together they collide a question none asks: at WHICH structural level is the delta computed?
selection.spark: P01-001 says advance only by the delta. P06-001 says recognition can happen at skeleton, blueprint, or simulation level. If you only advance by the delta, then the delta must itself be located at a specific structural depth — a skeleton-level delta is a different advance than a simulation-level delta.
selection.motive: The cushion distinguishes two cases: taking published work to a next level, versus building a feature from vague inspiration. This blend separates those by depth — a deeper inheritance level corresponds to a more radical advance.
selection.tension: P01-001's delta is binary and deterministic (one bit flips per level). P06-001's structural levels are a continuum of depth, not a sequence of bits. A binary delta cannot express 'advanced at the blueprint level but not the simulation level.'
selection.discovery_path: P01-001 says each advance = inherited content + one new delta. P06-001 says structural understanding has distinct depths (skeleton/blueprint/simulation). Combining: a delta is not a single bit but a CHANGE LOCATED AT A DEPTH — you can inherit a source's skeleton while replacing its blueprint, or inherit its blueprint while replacing its simulation. P04-004's 'extends' pointer then needs to record not just WHAT is extended but at WHAT DEPTH. Pursuing 'what does a depth-tagged delta enable' forced the thesis: the system can classify each advance by which structural layer it modifies, which directly distinguishes incremental from transformative advancement.

blends.thesis: Depth-located deltas: every advance the system surfaces is tagged by which structural layer it modifies — inheriting the skeleton but replacing the blueprint is a 'next-level' advance; inheriting skeleton AND blueprint but adding a new simulation is incremental; replacing the skeleton itself is a domain leap. The depth of the delta IS the measure of how far the work has been advanced.
blends.mechanism: When a resonance hit is scored, the articulation layer identifies which of the three layers the new contribution touches relative to the anchor. It records an 'extends-at-depth-N' pointer. The user can then filter: 'show me only blueprint-level advances on my published source' or 'show me skeleton-level leaps for my vague feature idea.' Accumulated pointers form a layered map showing not just what the concept became, but at which depths it has grown.
blends.emergent_structure: A delta has a depth coordinate, and the depth coordinate is a measure of advancement magnitude. Neither source has this: P01-001's delta is dimensionless (one bit); P06-001's levels describe how much the system UNDERSTANDS, not how much it CHANGES.
blends.advances_cushion: Cleanly separates the cushion's two stated cases (advance published work vs. build a feature from vague inspiration) by making 'how far above the existing level' a readable depth-coordinate, so the user can steer toward incremental refinement or radical reframing on demand.

source_cards.spark: A mathematics learning system that maps which skills must come before which others reveals how a sparse prerequisite graph can guide traversal without ever specifying the destination — the structure biases the path without determining it.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the same role the prerequisite graph plays here: it is a minimal structure that biases movement without dictating a route. The Gagné graph never tells the traversal engine which branch to take globally — it only tells it what the valid next step is from the current position, which is structurally identical to how a three-point anchor biases a wandering agent without pre-specifying what it will find. The 'local coherence, global unpredictability' match is the most useful point of contact: within any Gagné branch the next move is determinate, but the overall path is emergent because it depends on the learner's actual state — your agents face the same shape, where each hop is locally coherent with the anchor's pull but the aggregate trajectory cannot be predicted before traversal begins. The 'inheritance without repetition' property is also directly applicable: the Gagné system never re-tests what a lower node already confirmed, which models how a wandering agent might avoid re-covering ground the anchor has already resolved.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills as a directed graph where each concept is a prerequisite for the one above it. To assess a learner, the system walks this graph from wherever the learner currently stands, inheriting what is already known and testing only what has not yet been resolved. The path is locally determined at each step — the next node to visit is always the nearest unresolved prerequisite — but which branch activates depends entirely on where the learner sits right now, so the actual traversal route is emergent rather than fixed in advance.
source_cards.use: Consider designing your agents' traversal so that each hop inherits a 'what has already been confirmed relevant' state from prior hops — a lightweight prerequisite record that prevents the agent from circling back to already-resolved proximity, keeping the wander genuinely progressive rather than redundant.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné hierarchy is a closed, fully pre-authored graph: every node and every edge exists before traversal begins, which is the structural opposite of what your system requires. Your agents must be able to arrive somewhere the system did not know existed when it launched — Gagné's design forecloses that entirely. The mismatch on productive incompleteness is equally sharp: the paper's explicit goal is to eliminate incompleteness as fast and precisely as possible, treating any unresolved gap as a defect to be closed. Your system's generative power depends on holding incompleteness intact as the tension that makes wandering productive, so the resolution drive that makes Gagné work is precisely what your system must not replicate.

source_cards.spark: A method for diagnosing learning gaps in mathematics uses 'inheritance coding' — a binary map of which skills depend on which — and that dependency structure turns out to share a skeleton with how your system tracks what a concept has already absorbed before it can reach the next insight.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the role of the hierarchy's root skill: it is the thing everything else must stay in relationship with, the fixed point that gives the traversal its direction. The wandering agent's sparse inputs — a few resonant signals pulled from distant domains — mirror the binary inheritance vector: a minimal encoding that nonetheless carries enough structural information to locate where in a vast space something meaningful sits. Where Gagné's system reads the vector and asks 'which rung is missing,' your system reads the resonance signal and asks 'which structural match is hiding.' Both are navigating a large space with a deliberately compressed representation, using inheritance — what the current state carries forward from prior states — to avoid re-scanning everything from scratch.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills in a strict bottom-up chain: you cannot reliably master long division until you have mastered subtraction, and the system encodes this as a binary inheritance vector — a compact record of which lower skills are present or absent. Diagnosing where a learner is stuck means reading that vector and finding the lowest unmastered rung. The hierarchy is built once, by experts, before any learner touches it — the structure is fixed, and navigation through it is deterministic.
source_cards.use: You might treat each confirmed anchor connection as a node that 'inherits' the structural properties of the anchor, letting subsequent wanders start from that node rather than from zero — so the system accumulates a sparse but growing map of which conceptual territories are already in harmonic relationship with the user's work, and new agents can bias their traversal away from already-covered ground.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné hierarchy is pre-built by domain experts and is fully deterministic: every prerequisite relationship is declared in advance, and the path through it is fixed before any learner arrives. Your system has no pre-existing taxonomy — the structure only becomes visible after the wander, retrospectively, as resonance is scored. This means the 'inheritance' in your system is a soft, emergent pattern discovered in chaos, not a hard prerequisite chain enforced by design. Any technique borrowed from Gagné that assumes a stable, expert-defined graph will break the moment it meets your system's refusal to commit to a fixed topology before the fact.

source_cards.spark: A system that diagnoses where a student is stuck — from just a few test answers — and maps the entire prerequisite chain they need to climb shares a structural ghost with your system: sparse input producing rich navigational output. The surprise is that 'where you are' can imply 'what must come before' without anyone spelling out the path.
source_cards.bridge: Your system takes an anchor — a concept or work-in-progress — and tries to surface what would genuinely advance it. The structural echo is here: the anchor is the student's current position, and the question your system is implicitly asking is the same one Gagné's hierarchy asks — what must be understood first for the next idea to land? The 'inheritance' in the source is prerequisite dependency; in your system, that same force could operate as a navigational constraint, not on learning sequences, but on conceptual adjacency — what a person must grasp before a new domain's structure becomes useful to them rather than merely novel. The sparse-to-rich movement maps cleanly: a thin anchor description generates a wide navigational space, just as a few test answers generate a full diagnostic tree.
source_cards.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills into a strict dependency tree: you cannot meaningfully learn long division until you have internalized subtraction, and you cannot internalize subtraction until you have grasped counting. Inheritance coding encodes these dependencies formally — each higher skill carries a binary record of every lower skill it requires. Feed the system a small set of test responses, and it can infer not just what the student got wrong, but the entire upstream chain of gaps that explains the failure. Sparse evidence, rich map.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your system could score candidate connections not just by structural similarity to the anchor, but by conceptual prerequisite distance — surfacing ideas the user is already positioned to absorb, before surfacing ideas that would require too many intermediate leaps to be actionable.
source_cards.limit: The Gagné hierarchy is prescriptive and assumes one correct sequence of mastery; your system is built on the premise that trails are unpredictable and only make sense in retrospect. The inheritance in the source is exact — binary codes, deterministic paths — while your resonance scoring is deliberately vague and structural. Most critically, the source's purpose is gap-closing: it identifies what is missing and tells you how to fill it in order. Your purpose is gap-exploiting: the creative leap lives precisely in the unexpected crossing, not the orderly climb. A closed, cumulative system designed to eliminate surprise is almost the opposite of an open, emergent one designed to produce it.

selection.why_these_cards: S9cd764-001 gives local-coherence/global-unpredictability traversal (each hop determinate, aggregate emergent). P05-005 gives the accumulating sparse map of already-covered territory. P02-003 gives prerequisite distance — surfacing what the user is positioned to absorb before what requires too many leaps. Three cards about navigation, in tension over whether the system should avoid covered ground, seek reachable ground, or wander freely.
selection.spark: P02-003 says surface ideas the user can already absorb (short prerequisite distance). But S9cd764-001's whole value is unpredictable distant leaps. If you only surface what's already absorbable, you've recreated the Gagné climb the system was built to escape. Yet a leap too far is 'merely novel, not actionable.' There must be a reachability frontier.
selection.motive: The cushion's risk is producing connections so distant the user can't act on them, or so close they add nothing. This blend builds the mechanism that targets the actionable frontier — far enough to be new, near enough to be usable.
selection.tension: P02-003 wants short prerequisite distance (absorbability). S9cd764-001 wants long, surprising trajectories (novelty). P05-005 wants to push AWAY from covered ground (anti-redundancy). 'Push away from covered' and 'stay absorbable' pull in opposite directions — the absorbable region is adjacent to the covered region.
selection.discovery_path: P02-003 says actionable = low prerequisite distance from the anchor. P05-005 says the system accumulates a map of territory already in harmonic relationship with the user's work. S9cd764-001 says trajectories should be locally coherent but globally surprising. Holding all three: the actionable zone is NOT close to the anchor — it is close to the EDGE of the accumulated map (one prerequisite-hop beyond confirmed territory). Pursuing 'where exactly is the frontier' forced the thesis: the system should target connections that are one absorbable hop beyond the growing boundary of already-resonant territory, not one hop from the original anchor. The frontier moves outward as the map grows, so surprise and absorbability stop being in conflict.

blends.thesis: The expanding absorbability frontier: the system targets resonance hits that are exactly one prerequisite-hop beyond the EDGE of the accumulated map of already-resonant territory — not one hop from the original anchor. As confirmed connections accumulate, the frontier migrates outward, so the same 'one absorbable hop' criterion produces progressively more distant and surprising material over a session without ever becoming unactionable.
blends.mechanism: Maintain the growing sparse map (P05-005). For each candidate, compute prerequisite distance not to the anchor but to the nearest confirmed node on the frontier. Score highest those that are one hop beyond the current boundary — reachable from where the user now stands (because they've absorbed the intervening nodes), yet outside everything confirmed so far. Early in a session the frontier hugs the anchor (safe, close advances); late in a session it has migrated far, so the same rule now surfaces genuinely distant cross-domain leaps that have become absorbable because of the path traveled to get there.
blends.emergent_structure: Absorbability is measured against a MOVING boundary, not the fixed anchor — so a connection's actionability depends on the session's accumulated history, not just on the connection itself. No source card has a frontier that migrates: P02-003 measures distance from the fixed anchor, P05-005 only avoids covered ground, S9cd764-001 has no absorbability criterion at all.
blends.advances_cushion: Resolves the core tension of an advancement system — staying useful while pushing past the current level — by letting the reachable-but-new zone travel outward as the user's understanding compounds, so the system advances the work further and further without ever handing the user a leap they can't take.

references.title: Sparse, Dense, and Attentional Representations for Text Retrieval

adjacent.resemblance: The bi-encoder/cross-encoder two-stage retrieval architecture is a close cousin: a lightweight, lower-information encoder handles first-stage traversal for breadth, while a richer cross-encoder handles scoring for precision. This is structurally the same asymmetric-density split applied to the same retrieval pipeline.
adjacent.still_new: The existing architecture uses asymmetric encoders for efficiency reasons (speed vs. accuracy trade-off), not as a tunable 'advancement distance' dial. No hit frames the GAP between the two representations as a controllable parameter that governs how far from the user's current level the system reaches. The semantic reinterpretation of the density gap as an advancement-distance knob — and the deliberate widening/narrowing of it as a design choice rather than an engineering constraint — is not present in any hit.
adjacent.reasoning: The bi-encoder/cross-encoder split performs nearly the same structural transfer (sparse traversal anchor, rich scoring anchor on the same object), making this ADJACENT rather than NOVEL. The unprecedented element is the reframing of the density gap as a semantically meaningful, user-facing advancement-distance parameter rather than a latency/accuracy engineering trade-off.

references.title: Knowledge Graph-based Session Recommendation with Adaptive ...
Knowledge graph-based adaptive recommendation model for ...

adjacent.resemblance: ZPD defines absorbability as a moving boundary relative to current learner state, not a fixed origin — this is the same core transfer. Knowledge-graph session recommendation with adaptive propagation updates the recommendation frontier based on within-session accumulated interactions. Together these are close cousins: a migrating absorbability boundary measured against accumulated session state on a prerequisite graph.
adjacent.still_new: No hit combines the ZPD migrating-boundary logic with prerequisite-hop distance measured to the NEAREST CONFIRMED FRONTIER NODE (rather than to the fixed anchor or to the learner's global state). The specific mechanism — frontier migration driven by confirmed resonance nodes, with one-hop distance computed to the frontier edge rather than to the origin — is not performed by any hit. ZPD does not operate on a sparse confirmed-node map; session recommendation does not use prerequisite-hop distance to a migrating frontier boundary.
adjacent.reasoning: ZPD and session-adaptive knowledge-graph recommendation each perform a genuinely close cousin of this move (migrating boundary, session-accumulated state), placing this firmly ADJACENT rather than NOVEL. The still-new element is the precise mechanism: one-hop distance measured to the nearest confirmed frontier node on a sparse resonance map, causing the same criterion to produce progressively more distant material as the session accumulates.

references.title: [PDF] Category Theory: an abstract setting for analogy and comparison

novel.resemblance: SME performs structure-mapping from a pre-specified base to a target; anti-unification infers a least-general generalization from two given, already-formal examples; category-theoretic analogy uses colimit-style constructions to relate specified structures. All three share components: generalization inference, cross-domain matching, and structural alignment.
novel.still_new: No hit performs the specific move: treating resonance detection as the TRIGGER that retroactively licenses formalization of the anchor, with the matched source acting as the chisel. SME and anti-unification both require both inputs to be sufficiently formal before the algorithm runs. The colimit is always computed forward from given objects. The inversion — formalization as output of matching, not precondition for it — appears in no hit.
novel.reasoning: The evidence confirms that the ingredients (colimit logic, anti-unification, structure-mapping) exist and are well-studied, but every hit applies them to already-specified inputs. The specific transfer — using a resonance hit to retroactively reconstruct the minimal skeleton that explains the user's vague anchor — is not performed by any hit. Component overlap is high; move overlap is zero.

references.title: Levels of Abstraction in Ontology Design - ResearchGate
Extending the Hierarchy of System Specifications and Morphisms ...

novel.resemblance: Ontology abstraction hierarchies describe levels of structural depth; system specification hierarchies (DEVS-style) organize knowledge from behavior to structure; delta learning computes parameter-level changes between models. All share the ingredients of layered abstraction and change quantification.
novel.still_new: No hit performs the specific move: assigning a DEPTH COORDINATE to a knowledge delta such that the depth coordinate itself serves as the measure of advancement magnitude — and exposing that coordinate as a user-facing filter for 'how far has this work been advanced.' Ontology levels describe where a concept sits, not how much a contribution changes it. Delta learning measures parameter distance, not structural-layer distance. The typed-delta-as-advancement-metric move is absent from all hits.
novel.reasoning: The evidence provides components (abstraction layers, delta computation, hierarchical specifications) but no hit combines them into the specific transfer: tagging a contribution by which structural layer it modifies and treating that tag as the primary measure of advancement magnitude. The move is unprecedented in the evidence.

hits.title: Zone of proximal development - Wikipedia
hits.snippet: # Zone of proximal development. The **zone of proximal development** (**ZPD**) is a concept in educational psychology that represents the space between what a learner is capable of doing unsupported and what the learner cannot do even with support. It is the range where the learner is able to perform, but only with support from a teacher or a peer with more knowledge or expertise. Following Vygots…

hits.snippet: # Deep Exploration for Recommendation Systems. Modern recommendation systems ought to benefit by probing for and learning from delayed feedback. Research has tended to focus on learning from a user’s response to a single recommendation.
